prompt: Construct a FleetCommerce Operations Hub supporting an offline-first dealership parts and vehicle catalog workflow, where staff can manage vehicle content, build carts, place orders, and stay informed through a unified messaging and alerting experience. Primary roles include Administrator, Inventory Manager, Sales Associate, and Auditor. In the Templ-rendered web UI, users sign in with a local username and password, land on a dashboard showing open carts, active orders, and unread notifications, and can switch between Vehicle Catalog, Cart, Orders, and Notification Center without page reload surprises by using clear success/error banners and inline validation messages. Vehicle information management supports structured browsing by brand, series, and model, with image/video attachments, draft edits, and publish/unpublish controls; bulk import/export is offered as local CSV files with preview and row-level error marking before commit. The cart and order flow supports merging carts by customer account, automatically flagging invalid items (discontinued, not published, or stock at zero), and capturing order notes for picking and delivery. Orders follow a visible state timeline from creation to payment-recorded, cutoff, picking, arrival, pickup or delivery, and completion, with partial out-of-stock handling that allows backorder lines and optional order split for immediate fulfillment. The Messaging & Notification Center consolidates in-app messages and system announcements with read/unread states, bulk mark-as-read, and user subscription preferences; "email/SMS/webhook" are represented as optional local export queues for later processing, with templated messages that render variables like order number, promised date, and location. On the system side, the backend uses Gin to expose REST-style endpoints to the Templ frontend for authentication, catalog edits, cart operations, order transitions, metric definitions, and notifications, with PostgreSQL as the system of record for transactional integrity and auditability. The order state machine is enforced server-side: invalid transitions are rejected, cutoff occurs automatically 30 minutes after creation unless payment-recorded is posted by an authorized role, and every state change writes an immutable operation audit trail including actor, timestamp, and before/after values. Inventory alerts are rule-based and evaluated on a schedule (for example every 15 minutes) and on key events; default thresholds include low stock under 5 units, overstock above 250 units, and near-expiry within 14 days, generating alerts that run through a closed-loop lifecycle of claim, process, and close with mandatory resolution notes. Metric framework and semantic layer management persists a unified Metric Store with versioned definitions, dimensions, time grain, filters, derived metrics, and window calculations; definition changes require impact analysis before activation, track lineage to dependent charts, and enforce metric-level permissions. Security and privacy remain local-only: passwords are salted and hashed, sensitive fields such as customer phone numbers are masked in the UI and encrypted at rest, file uploads are validated by type and size (for example max 25 MB) and stored on local disk with SHA-256 fingerprints, and all notification send logs and retries are maintained locally without relying on any external network services.
